title: notation

options.label: P1
options.author: gemma4:latest
options.text:
PREFIX (S-expression).

This notation yields the highest degree of machine-interpretability and parsing simplicity because it naturally maps to nested list structures (S-expressions). This allows the entire program to be treated as a homogenous, recursive data type (a tree), which simplifies the parsing process into a single, unambiguous mechanism (e.g., recursive descent parser). It avoids the complexity of operator precedence and associativity rules inherent in infix notation, and while postfix is stack-based, prefix notation directly models the hierarchical, function-call structure fundamental to defining computation graphs, adhering best to the minimal core principle.

options.label: P2
options.author: llama3:latest
options.text:
PREFIX (S-expression)

Since the meta-axiom requires that every feature must be expressible in terms of the core primitives without additional syntax or semantic rules, we should choose a surface notation that allows for efficient parsing and minimizes the need for syntactic analysis. PREFIX (S-expression) achieves this by representing expressions as prefix trees, where the operator is at the root and its arguments are at the leaves. This allows for simple and unambiguous parsing, making it the most machine-interpretable option. Additionally, S-expressions have been extensively used in Lisp-like languages and have a well-established syntax, making them a familiar choice for many programmers.

options.label: P3
options.author: qwen3:8b
options.text:
PREFIX  
Prefix notation (S-expressions) offers unambiguous hierarchical structure, enabling straightforward recursive parsing without precedence rules. Infix requires complex precedence/associativity parsing, postfix demands stack-based interpretation, and prefix minimizes ambiguity while reusing list-based primitives.

options.label: P4
options.author: deepseek-r1:8b
options.text:
PREFIX  
Prefix notation (S-expressions) provides unambiguous parsing by eliminating operator precedence and associativity rules. It directly maps to a recursive function application structure, enabling simple recursive descent parsers and ensuring testable semantics. Postfix notation adds parsing complexity similar to infix, while infix requires explicit handling of precedence, violating the machine-interpretability-first axiom. Prefix reuses the functional application model with minimal core primitives.

options.label: P5
options.author: mistral:7b
options.text:
Prefix Notation (S-expression)

Justification:
Prefix notation is a simple and unambiguous syntax, allowing for easier machine interpretation as it follows a hierarchical structure where operators precede their operands. This reduces ambiguity during parsing as the order of operations is implicit in the structure itself. Additionally, prefix notation allows for easy composability and functional programming constructs which adhere to the design goals of minimal core primitives, consistent reuse of existing constructs, and predictable behavior. Backward compatibility can be maintained by providing conversion functions between infix and prefix notations if needed.
